glossary.def: A game in which the same players play a fixed one-shot stage game over and over, allowing strategies to condition on history. Repetition makes punishments credible, which is why cooperation that is impossible in a single play can become a stable equilibrium once the game repeats.

The weight a player places on payoffs in later rounds, governed by the discount factor δ. A long shadow (δ near 1) means future punishment outweighs the one-round gain from cheating, which is the lever that makes cooperation rational in repeated games.

The canonical two-player game in which defecting strictly dominates cooperating (T > R > P > S), so two rational one-shot players both defect and reach an outcome both would rather avoid. It is the cleanest demonstration of the conflict between individual and collective rationality.

The strategy that cooperates on the first move and thereafter copies the opponent's previous move. It won Axelrod's iterated-Prisoner's-Dilemma tournaments by being nice, retaliatory, forgiving, and clear — never beating any single opponent, yet scoring highest overall by eliciting mutual cooperation.

A strategy that, if adopted by an entire population, cannot be invaded by a small group of mutants playing anything else. Introduced by Maynard Smith and Price, it is a strict refinement of Nash equilibrium: every ESS is a Nash equilibrium, but not every Nash equilibrium is an ESS.

The differential equation governing how strategy frequencies change under selection: a strategy's share grows in proportion to how much its fitness exceeds the population average. Its stable rest points are exactly the evolutionarily stable strategies, and on Hawk–Dove it converges to the mixed ESS at Hawk fraction V/C.

A game in which players can form binding agreements and pool their efforts, described not by a payoff matrix but by a characteristic function v(S) giving the value each coalition S can secure on its own. The question shifts from what players will do to how the joint payoff should be divided fairly.

The unique fair division of a coalition's payoff, defined as each player's average marginal contribution over all orders in which the players could be assembled. It is the only allocation satisfying efficiency, symmetry, the null-player property, and additivity, and underlies SHAP feature attribution in machine learning.

Maynard Smith, J. & Price, G. R. (1973). The Logic of Animal Conflict. Nature 246.
Shapley, L. S. (1953). A Value for n-Person Games. In Contributions to the Theory of Games II, Princeton University Press.
Maynard Smith, J. (1982). Evolution and the Theory of Games. Cambridge University Press.
Friedman, J. W. (1971). A Non-cooperative Equilibrium for Supergames. Review of Economic Studies 38(1).
Lundberg, S. M. & Lee, S.-I. (2017). A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions. NeurIPS 30 (SHAP).
